section.title: Habakkuk's First Complaint and God's Answer
section.sectionRationale: The book opens not with a sermon to the people but with a prophet's raw complaint to God about the violence and injustice in Judah, and then God's shocking reply. This first exchange sets up the whole dialogue: it names the problem, and it names the terrifying answer, the Babylonians, that will drive the rest of the book.
section.thesis: Habakkuk cries out over the violence and lawlessness of God's own people, and God answers that He is already at work, raising up the ruthless Chaldeans, an answer so startling it only deepens the prophet's questions.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the prophet's complaint, how long will God tolerate violence and paralyzed justice; and the LORD's answer, look and be utterly astounded, for I am raising up the Chaldeans.

verses.text: This is the {{massa|burden}} that Habakkuk the prophet received in a vision:
How long, O LORD, must I call for help but You do not hear, or cry out to You, “{{chamas|Violence!}}” but You do not save?
Why do You make me see iniquity? Why do You tolerate wrongdoing? Destruction and violence are before me. Strife is ongoing, and conflict abounds.
Therefore the law is paralyzed, and justice never goes forth. For the wicked hem in the {{tsaddiq|righteous}}, so that justice is perverted.
“Look at the nations and observe, be utterly astounded! For I am doing a work in your days that you would never believe even if someone told you.
For behold, I am raising up the {{kasdim|Chaldeans}}, that ruthless and impetuous nation, which marches through the breadth of the earth to seize dwellings not their own.
They are dreaded and feared; from themselves they derive justice and sovereignty.
Their horses are swifter than leopards, fiercer than wolves of the night. Their horsemen charge ahead, and their cavalry comes from afar. They fly like a vulture, swooping down to devour.
All of them come bent on violence; their hordes advance like the east wind; they gather prisoners like sand.
They scoff at kings and make rulers an object of scorn. They laugh at every fortress and build up siege ramps to seize it.
Then they sweep by like the wind and pass through. They are guilty; their own strength is their god.”

massa.gloss: A burden, or a prophetic oracle. The word can mean a heavy load lifted up and carried, and it becomes a term for a weighty word from God. Habakkuk's message is literally a burden: a heavy revelation he must bear and then speak.

chamas.gloss: Violence, wrong, cruelty, the ruin that people inflict on one another. It is the same word used of the earth in the days of Noah, Genesis 6:11. Habakkuk shouts this one word at God as his whole complaint: the covenant people are drowning in violence.

tsaddiq.gloss: The righteous one, the person who is in the right and lives rightly before God. The wicked in Judah hem in the righteous so that justice is twisted. This same word anchors the book's great line, the righteous will live by faith, in 2:4.

kasdim.gloss: The Chaldeans, that is the Babylonians, the rising imperial power that would soon sweep across the Near East. God names them as the very instrument He is raising up, a rescuer's answer that sounds at first like a nightmare.

basic.context: Habakkuk is a prophet who lives in Judah near the end of the seventh century before Christ, as the old Assyrian empire fades and Babylon rises. He looks around at God's own people and sees violence, corruption, and courts where justice never works. So he does something unusual for a prophet: instead of preaching to the people, he takes his complaint straight to God. And God answers, but the answer is shocking: He is going to send the brutal Babylonians.
basic.hermeneutics: This opening is a real, honest prayer of protest, and the Bible includes it to show that faith can bring its hardest questions to God. Habakkuk is not being faithless; he is wrestling. The point is not that we should copy his exact words but that God welcomes the honest cry of a believer who cannot square what he sees with what he knows about God.
basic.originalLanguages: Habakkuk's message is a burden, massa, a heavy word he must carry. His one-word complaint is violence, chamas, the same word used of the world before the flood. The wicked crush the righteous, the tsaddiq, the very word that returns in the book's famous promise. And God's answer names the Chaldeans, the Babylonians, as the ones He is raising up.
basic.typology: The prophet who stands between a guilty people and a holy God, bearing their burden and pleading, points forward to Christ, the true prophet and intercessor who carries the sin of His people. And the long wait for God to act anticipates the greater waiting for the day when all wrongs are made right.
basic.christ: Habakkuk's cry, how long, is the cry of every sufferer, and it is answered fully only in Jesus, who enters our violent world, absorbs its worst, and will return to end it. God's word in verse 5, I am doing a work you would never believe, is quoted by Paul in Acts 13:41 as a warning about the even greater work God has done in Christ, a salvation many refuse to believe.
basic.god: We learn that God is not indifferent to violence and injustice; He sees it and He will act. We also learn that His ways are far higher than ours: His answer to sin can look, at first, like more trouble, yet He is sovereign even over pagan empires, using them for His purposes while still holding them guilty.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 13:41 quotes God's astonishing work here as a warning about the gospel. Psalm 13 and Psalm 73 model the same honest wrestling. Jeremiah and 2 Kings 24 describe the Babylonian invasion God announces.
basic.oneStory: The book opens on the problem the whole Bible addresses: a world, even God's own people, ruined by violence, and a God who seems silent. God's answer begins the long road that runs through judgment and exile toward the coming of Christ, in whom God does the work no one would have believed.

context.literary: The book begins as a dialogue rather than a typical prophetic address. Verse 1 is a superscription; then the prophet speaks to God in the form of a lament, how long and why, and God replies in verses 5 to 11. This question-and-answer shape drives the whole first two chapters.
context.historical: Habakkuk prophesies in Judah in the years around 605 before Christ, after the fall of Assyria and as Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar becomes the dominant power. Judah is inwardly corrupt and outwardly about to be overrun. The rising Chaldeans God names are the empire that will besiege and destroy Jerusalem in 586.
context.canonical: Habakkuk stands among the twelve Minor Prophets and is unique for taking the form of a sustained argument with God. Its central verse, 2:4, becomes one of the most quoted Old Testament texts in the New, shaping the doctrine of justification by faith.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record honestly the prophet's protest against the violence and injustice within Judah, and God's startling answer that He is raising up Babylon, so that the reader is drawn into the tension the rest of the book will resolve.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes Habakkuk's lament and God's specific historical answer to Judah. It does not prescribe that God will always send an empire, but it does model bringing honest questions to God, and it teaches the abiding truth that God sovereignly rules the nations.

debates.question: Is Habakkuk's complaint a sin of unbelief or an act of faith?

views.label: An act of faith
views.case: He brings his questions to God rather than abandoning Him; lament is a recognized form of biblical prayer, and God answers rather than rebukes him.

views.label: A wavering to be corrected
views.case: The sharp how long and why show real strain, and the book's journey is Habakkuk being brought from protest to trust, so his opening posture is not yet where God will lead him.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage shows that God receives the honest cry of His people and patiently leads them from confusion to faith.

study.originalLanguages: The prophet's message is a massa, a burden, a heavy oracle he must lift and carry. His complaint condenses to one shouted word, chamas, violence, which Scripture uses for the corruption that filled the earth before the flood; Judah has become that kind of place. The wicked hem in the tsaddiq, the righteous, the very term that will carry the book's climactic promise in 2:4. And God's reply centers on the Kasdim, the Chaldeans, the Babylonians He is deliberately raising up, whose strength has become their god.

typology.type: The prophet bearing the burden and interceding
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true and final prophet, who carries the burden of His people's sin and intercedes for them.

typology.type: The cry how long from a suffering saint
typology.fulfillment: Answered in Christ's coming and consummated at His return, when He wipes away every tear and ends all violence, Revelation 6:10 and 21:4.

study.christ: Habakkuk's protest voices the ache of every believer who longs for God to act against evil, and that longing is answered in Jesus. God's declaration in verse 5, I am doing a work in your days that you would never believe, is taken up by Paul in Acts 13:41 and applied to the gospel itself: God has done in Christ a saving work so staggering that many refuse to believe it. The prophet who stands in the gap between a sinful people and a holy God foreshadows the one Mediator, Jesus, who bore our violence and will one day silence it forever.
study.god: God is not deaf or distant, though He can seem so; He sees the violence and will answer in His time. He is sovereign over history, able to raise up and direct even a ruthless pagan empire to accomplish His purposes, yet without excusing that empire, for He calls the Chaldeans guilty whose own strength has become their god. His ways are higher than ours, and His answers often confound our expectations.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Habakkuk 1:5, the work you would never believe, as a warning to those tempted to reject the gospel.
How long, O LORD; the same lament posture of faith crying out to God.
The historical rise of Nebuchadnezzar and Babylon against Judah, the work God announces here.
God summons Babylon as His instrument of judgment on Judah, parallel to Habakkuk's oracle.

study.oneStory: This first exchange opens the wound the whole Bible sets out to heal: a world, and even God's covenant people, corrupted by violence, and a God who seems silent. God's answer sets history moving toward judgment, exile, and ultimately toward Christ, in whom God accomplishes the work no one would have believed, the salvation of sinners through the cross.

sermon.title: How Long and Why?
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Habakkuk, this message treats the prophet's opening complaint, Habakkuk 1:1-4, showing that God welcomes the honest questions of faith and remains at work even when He seems silent. A faithful, orthodox handling of the passage that opens this movement.

quiz.q: What is Habakkuk complaining about in his first cry to God?

options.text: The violence and injustice among God's own people, and God's apparent silence.
options.note: His complaint is the violence within Judah and courts where justice never works.

options.text: That the wicked oppress the righteous and justice is perverted in Judah.
options.note: True and part of his cry, but the fuller complaint is the pervasive violence and God's apparent silence.

options.text: That a foreign army has already destroyed Jerusalem.
options.note: The invasion is still future here; his complaint is about internal corruption.

options.text: That God has been too harsh and quick to punish.
options.note: The opposite: he protests that God seems slow to act against evil.

quiz.q: What is startling about God's answer in verses 5 to 11?

options.text: God is raising up the ruthless Babylonians as His instrument.
options.note: The answer to violence in Judah is a more violent empire, an answer no one would believe.

options.text: God says He is already doing a work so astonishing no one would believe it.
options.note: True and startling, but the specific shock is that the instrument is Babylon.

options.text: God promises to fix everything immediately and painlessly.
options.note: His answer is anything but painless; it sets up the prophet's next complaint.

options.text: God refuses to answer at all.
options.note: God does answer, and the answer itself is the shock.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament use God's words in verse 5?

options.text: Paul quotes it in Acts 13:41 as a warning about refusing the astonishing work of the gospel.
options.note: The work you would never believe is applied to God's saving work in Christ.

options.text: It is applied to the surprising, hard-to-believe work of God in salvation.
options.note: Right in substance, but the precise reference is Paul's warning in Acts 13:41.

options.text: Jesus quotes it about the destruction of the temple.
options.note: It is Paul in Acts who takes it up, applied to the gospel.

options.text: It is never referenced in the New Testament.
options.note: It is directly quoted in Acts 13:41.

quiz.q: What does this passage teach about God and the nations?

options.text: God sovereignly rules and even directs pagan empires, yet still holds them accountable.
options.note: He raises up the Chaldeans and calls them guilty; sovereignty and justice hold together.

options.text: God is able to use even a wicked nation to accomplish His purposes.
options.note: True, but it omits that He still holds that nation guilty for its evil.

options.text: God approves of everything Babylon does.
options.note: He uses them yet calls them guilty; He does not approve their violence.

options.text: God has no control over powerful nations like Babylon.
options.note: The passage says the opposite: God Himself is raising them up.

quiz.q: What does Habakkuk's honest lament model for us?

options.text: That faith can bring its hardest questions directly to God.
options.note: Lament is a form of prayer; God receives the honest cry of His people.

options.text: That God is patient with those who wrestle honestly with Him.
options.note: True and comforting, but the main point is that faith takes its questions to God.

options.text: That questioning God is always sinful unbelief.
options.note: His wrestling is within faith; God engages rather than rejects him.

options.text: That we should keep our doubts hidden from God.
options.note: Habakkuk does the opposite, and God answers him.

questions: Habakkuk brought his hardest questions straight to God rather than walking away. What questions or frustrations do you need to bring honestly to God instead of burying them?
The prophet cried how long over violence and injustice he could not fix. Where are you waiting on God for something that feels unbearably slow, and what would it look like to keep praying anyway?
God's answer was not what Habakkuk expected or wanted. When has God answered you in a way that confused you before it comforted you?
God is sovereign even over ruthless powers like Babylon. How does it steady you to know that no nation, leader, or crisis is outside God's control?
Paul applied God's astonishing work to the gospel itself. Where do you struggle to believe that God is truly at work, and how might the cross reassure you?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered burden, massa, is also a standard term for a prophetic oracle; it carries the sense of a weighty word the prophet must lift up and deliver.
The Septuagint reads Look, you scoffers, wonder and perish, the form Paul cites in Acts 13:41. The Hebrew reads look among the nations and be astounded.
The Chaldeans are the Babylonians; the BSB footnote makes this identification explicit. The original em dash in some editions is rendered here as a clean clause break.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Habakkuk 1:1-11. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. Original em dashes in verses 5 and 6 rendered as commas or clean clause breaks per style; no em or en dashes used.
